qtype: basic operations and fractions from data
tex:
20 students choose their favourite science subject.
\newline
The results are shown in the bar chart.
\newline

%\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

(a) Work out how many more students choose biology than physics.
\newline

(b) Write down the fraction of students whose favourite science subject is chemistry.
\newline

(c) One of the 20 students is picked at random.
\newline
Write down the probability that this student did not choose biology.
\newline

(d) Only one of the averages, median, mode and mean can be found for these results.
\newline

(i) (e) Write down the average that can be found.
\newline

(f) Find this average for these results.
\newline

(g) Explain why the range cannot be found.
\newline

(h) The results are to be shown in a pie chart.
\newline

(i) (i) Complete the table.
\newline

%\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

(j) Complete the pie chart.
\newline

%\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

content.content:
Work out how many more students choose biology than physics.
\newline

\newline

One of the 20 students is picked at random.
\newline

\newline

Only one of the averages, median, mode and mean can be found for these results.
\newline

Write down the average that can be found.
\newline

Find this average for these results.
\newline

Explain why the range cannot be found.
\newline

The results are to be shown in a pie chart.
\newline

qtype: percentage calculation, temperature change, money calculation
tex:
A family go on a skiing holiday to America.
\newline

(a) The hotel has 840 rooms.
\newline
735 rooms are occupied.
\newline

Calculate the percentage of rooms that are occupied.
\newline

(b) The temperature in the hotel is $21^\circ$C.
\newline
The temperature in the hotel is $26.7^\circ$C warmer than at the top of the mountain.
\newline
The temperature at the top of the mountain is $3.2^\circ$C colder than at the bottom of the mountain.
\newline

Work out the temperature at the bottom of the mountain.
\newline

(c) %\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

There are two adults and one child in the family.
\newline
They hire all their equipment.
\newline
The child skis for 3 days and snowboards for 4 days.
\newline
Both adults ski for 7 days.
\newline
All three of them hire a helmet for 7 days.
\newline

Work out the total cost of the equipment hire for the family.
\newline

\$ 

(d) A ski lift, when full, takes 4000 passengers per hour.
\newline
This lift works for 10 hours a day.
\newline
One day, this lift is 90\% full for 3 hours and 75\% full for 7 hours.
\newline

Work out the number of passengers who take the lift that day.
\newline

(e) The family buy their lift passes before the holiday for a total of 51 400 rupees.
\newline
In America, the passes cost a total of \$684.
\newline
The exchange rate is 1 rupee = \$0.0129.
\newline

Show that the family save 1620 rupees, correct to the nearest 10 rupees, by buying the passes
before their holiday.
\newline

(f) The height, $h$ metres, of the mountain is 2642 m, correct to the nearest metre.
\newline

Complete this statement about the value of $h$.
\newline

 $\le h <$

stem.content:
A family go on a skiing holiday to America.
\newline

_rawBody:
A family go on a skiing holiday to America.

\begin{parts}
\item The hotel has 840 rooms.
735 rooms are occupied.

Calculate the percentage of rooms that are occupied.

\AnswerLine[][\%]
\Marks{1}

\item The temperature in the hotel is $21^\circ$C.
The temperature in the hotel is $26.7^\circ$C warmer than at the top of the mountain.
The temperature at the top of the mountain is $3.2^\circ$C colder than at the bottom of the mountain.

Work out the temperature at the bottom of the mountain.

\AnswerLine[][$^\circ$C]
\Marks{2}

\item
%\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    \relincludegraphics{Figures/Q02-HireTable.png}
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

There are two adults and one child in the family.
They hire all their equipment.
The child skis for 3 days and snowboards for 4 days.
Both adults ski for 7 days.
All three of them hire a helmet for 7 days.

Work out the total cost of the equipment hire for the family.

\$ \AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{2}

\item A ski lift, when full, takes 4000 passengers per hour.
This lift works for 10 hours a day.
One day, this lift is 90\% full for 3 hours and 75\% full for 7 hours.

Work out the number of passengers who take the lift that day.

\AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{3}

\item The family buy their lift passes before the holiday for a total of 51 400 rupees.
In America, the passes cost a total of \$684.
The exchange rate is 1 rupee = \$0.0129.

Show that the family save 1620 rupees, correct to the nearest 10 rupees, by buying the passes
before their holiday.

\answerlines[2]
\Marks{3}

\item The height, $h$ metres, of the mountain is 2642 m, correct to the nearest metre.

Complete this statement about the value of $h$.

\AnswerLine[][] $\le h <$ \AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{2}
\end{parts}

tex:
The scale drawing shows the positions of Kendra's house, $K$, and Latika's house, $L$, on a map.
\newline

%\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

(a) Jesminder's house, $J$, is on a bearing of $036^\circ$ from $K$ and on a bearing of $284^\circ$ from $L$.
\newline

Mark the position of $J$ on the map.
\newline

(b) The actual distance between $K$ and $L$ is 9600 metres.
\newline

(i) (c) Complete the scale of the map.
\newline

1 cm represents  metres

(d) Kendra walks from $K$ to $L$ at a constant speed of 4.5 km/h.
\newline
She leaves $K$ at 10 15.
\newline

Work out the time she arrives at $L$.
\newline

(e) (i) (f) Kendra and Latika leave Latika's house at 15 00 to go to the cinema.
\newline

\begin{subsubparts}

(g) They walk for 20 minutes at a constant speed of 4.5 km/h.
\newline

Work out the distance they walk.
\newline

(h) After walking for 20 minutes, they then run a distance of 6 km at a constant speed for
40 minutes.
\newline

Draw their journey to the cinema on the travel graph.
\newline

%\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

\end{subsubparts}

(i) Kendra and Latika leave the cinema at 18 05.
\newline
They travel back to Latika's house in a taxi at a constant speed of 30 km/h.
\newline

Complete the travel graph.
\newline

qtype: money calculation, ratio in simplest form
tex:
Prakash buys 45 flowers from a shop.
\newline

(a) %\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

Each bunch has 5 flowers.
\newline
The price of one bunch of flowers is \$2.68.
\newline

Using the special offer, work out how much Prakash pays for the 45 flowers.
\newline

(b) 15 of the flowers are red.
\newline
18 of the flowers are orange.
\newline
The rest of the flowers are yellow.
\newline

Write down the ratio of the number of red flowers : orange flowers : yellow flowers.
\newline
Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline

 :  : 

(c) Prakash gives the 45 flowers to his family.
\newline
He gives his grandmother $x$ flowers.
\newline
He gives his mother 8 more flowers than his grandmother.
\newline
He gives his cousin 6 fewer flowers than his grandmother.
\newline
He gives his sister twice as many flowers as he gives his cousin.
\newline

(i) (d) Use this information to show that $5x - 10 = 45$.
\newline

(e) Solve the equation $5x - 10 = 45$.
\newline

(f) Find the number of flowers that Prakash gives to his cousin.
\newline

tex:

\newline

(b) A recipe uses 550 g of flour to make 8 cakes.
\newline
Work out the amount of flour needed to make 360 cakes.
\newline
Give your answer in kilograms.
\newline

(c) %\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

Work out which bag of flour is the best value.
\newline
Show all your working.
\newline

(d) One cake costs 24 cents to make.
\newline
The baker sells each cake for 65 cents.
\newline

Calculate the percentage profit the baker makes on each cake.
\newline

(e) The baker asks some customers if they like lemon cake ($L$) and if they like chocolate cake ($C$).
\newline
The Venn diagram shows the results.
\newline

%\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

\newline

$n(\xi)$ =

\newline

\newline

$\{\text{Jai}, \text{Nera}\}$ =
